intro.description: This cheat sheet guides you through using the JavaHelper View to inspect variable type bindings in your Java workspace and detect inconsistent naming conventions.

item.title: Open the JavaHelper View
item.description:
Open the JavaHelper View from the Sandbox category:
1. Choose Window → Show View → Other…
2. Expand the Sandbox category
3. Select JavaHelper and click Open
The view displays a table of variable name and type binding information for Java elements in the workspace.
Click Perform to open the Show View dialog.

item.title: Inspect Variable Bindings
item.description:
The JavaHelper View table shows:
• Name — the variable or field name
• Type — the declared type
• Widest Type — the widest compatible type inferred from actual usage (e.g. List instead of ArrayList)
• Location — the source file and line number
When the Type column differs from the Widest Type column, the variable is declared with a more specific type than needed — a possible sign of inconsistent or unnecessarily tight coupling.

item.title: Sort and Filter Results
item.description:
Use the table controls to focus on relevant results:
• Click a column header to sort by that column
• Use the filter field at the top of the view to narrow results by name or type
• Double-click a row to navigate to the declaration in the Java editor
Focus on rows where the Widest Type column suggests a broader interface or abstract type — these are candidates for type widening refactoring.

item.title: Apply Type Widening
item.description:
To widen the declared type of a variable:
1. Double-click a row in the JavaHelper View to navigate to the declaration
2. Place the cursor on the type in the declaration
3. Press Alt+Shift+R (Rename) or use Refactor → Change Method Signature… to update the type
4. Replace the specific type (e.g. ArrayList) with the wider type (e.g. List)
Run your tests after refactoring to confirm that the widened type is compatible with all usages.
